pattern: Since distinct positive even numbers grow at least as fast as 2, 4, 6, ..., the largest count you can ever fit under a fixed total is capped by how many of those smallest possible even numbers it takes to exceed the total, and any leftover just gets folded into the last one.
hints: A sum of any distinct positive even numbers is always even, so an odd target can never be split at all.
Greedily take 2, then 4, then 6, and so on, stopping the moment taking the next one would blow past the target -- that greedy run is provably the longest possible list you can build.

Whatever amount is left over after that greedy run can simply be added onto the last number taken, since that keeps every number even, positive, and still strictly the largest (and therefore still unique).

edge_inputs.description: an odd total can never be split into even numbers
a total whose greedy run has no leftover to fold in
original worked example with an exact greedy run
original worked example that is odd and has no split
original worked example with leftover folded into the last term
a small total whose greedy run stops after just one number
the maximum allowed total minus one, which is odd

stress.description: random totals across the full bound, mixing even and odd
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    hi = max(1, min(size, 10000000000))
    final_sum = rng.randint(1, hi)
    return [final_sum]
